items.name: Habit Stacking (Atomic Habits)
items.description: Link new habits to existing routines: 'After I X, I will Y'
items.best_for: Building new habits when you have established routines
items.pros: Leverages existing neural pathways, automatic, requires no motivation
items.cons: Takes 1-2 weeks to establish, new habit depends on old one
items.example: After I pour coffee, I will review today's 3 priorities.

items.name: Implementation Intentions
items.description: Pre-decide when and where you'll do the habit: 'I will [behavior] at [time] in [location]'
items.best_for: Habits that happen at specific times
items.pros: Research-backed (2-3x more likely to follow through), removes decision-making
items.cons: Rigid — doesn't adapt well to schedule changes
items.example: I will exercise at 7 AM in my living room.

items.name: Temptation Bundling
items.description: Pair something you want to do with something you need to do
items.pros: Creates immediate reward, makes hard habits attractive
items.cons: Can become dependent on the bundled reward
items.example: I only listen to my favorite podcast while exercising.

items.name: Environment Design
items.description: Modify your physical space to make good habits easy and bad habits hard
items.best_for: Habits that are blocked by friction or temptation
items.pros: Removes willpower from the equation, works passively
items.cons: Requires initial setup effort, may not work for all habits
items.example: Put gym clothes by the door, delete social media apps from phone.

items.name: Accountability Partner
items.description: Share your habit goals with someone and check in regularly
items.best_for: Habits where you tend to quit when no one's watching
items.pros: Social pressure increases follow-through (65% more likely)
items.cons: Requires finding the right partner, can feel awkward
items.example: Text your accountability partner every time you complete your habit.

comparisons.insight: The best habit-building strategy combines multiple methods: Environment Design to reduce friction, Habit Stacking to trigger the behavior, and Accountability to maintain consistency. Start with one method and add others as needed.

keyInsight: Habits are more powerful than motivation because they run on autopilot. Design the Cue → Routine → Reward loop, use the Two-Minute Rule to start small, and combine Environment Design + Habit Stacking + Accountability for maximum consistency.
